name: Impossible Travel
title: Impossible travel: {user} logged in from two countries in 20 minutes
description: Account {user} authenticated from a corporate IP (United States) and then from {attacker_ip} ({country2}) 20 minutes later — physically impossible travel.
objective: Verify whether the geographically impossible logon pair for {user} indicates account compromise, or has a benign explanation such as VPN egress or mobile carrier routing.
checklist: Locate both logon events for {user} and note the timestamps and source IPs
Confirm the geolocation and ownership of each source IP
Rule out benign explanations: corporate VPN egress points, cloud proxy, mobile networks
Check what the foreign session did after authenticating (mailbox rules, file access)
Check for recent phishing-related events targeting {user}
Disposition and recommend session revocation if suspicious
hints: Search index=auth user={user} and compare the two source IPs — one is RFC1918-adjacent corporate space, one is a foreign ISP.
The classic benign cause is a VPN whose egress is geo-located abroad. Check if {attacker_ip} belongs to your VPN provider's ranges (in this environment, it does not).
Look at what the foreign session accessed — mass file access right after logon suggests a real compromise, not a geo-IP artifact.
expectedEvidence: Two authentication events for {user} from different countries within 20 minutes
Foreign IP is not a known VPN/proxy egress
Suspicious post-logon activity from the foreign session
User confirmation (simulated) that they did not travel
explanation: This is a genuine account compromise, most likely via phished or replayed credentials/session token. Impossible-travel alerts have a high false-positive rate in real SOCs (VPNs, mobile carriers, cloud proxies), which is why the checklist forces you to rule those out first. The discriminators here: the foreign IP is a residential ISP not associated with any corporate egress, and the session immediately enumerated files — behavior inconsistent with the user's normal pattern. Correct response: revoke all sessions, reset credentials, enforce MFA re-registration, and review accessed data.
reportTemplate: 1. Revoke all active sessions for {user}. 2. Force credential reset and MFA re-enrollment. 3. Review files accessed by the session from {attacker_ip}. 4. Add {attacker_ip} to the watchlist.

events.message: Authentication success user={user} src={src_ip} geo=US/Chicago mfa=passed app=email
Authentication success user={user} src={attacker_ip} geo={country2} mfa=legacy_bypass app=email
Resource access user={user} src={attacker_ip} resource=sharepoint action=list_files folder=/Finance
